persona.name: Tomoko Hayashi
persona.role: Grant writer for a university research lab
persona.core_preferences: Compelling scientific narrative. Clear significance and innovation sections. Accessible to interdisciplinary reviewers who may not be domain experts.
persona.hidden_preferences: The significance section should tell a STORY, not list facts — the reader should feel the gap in knowledge growing more urgent with each sentence until your proposed work feels like the obvious next step; significance sections that read like literature reviews miss the point. Specific aims should be independent enough that failing one doesn't sink the proposal, but connected enough that completing all of them produces something greater than the sum — reviewers look for this balance and penalize both disconnected aims and fragile dependency chains. When referencing prior work or pilot data, it should be framed as PROOF OF FEASIBILITY — the message is 'we can do this' not 'we already did this'; over-presenting what's already done makes reviewers wonder what's left to fund. The innovation section should name exactly what's new AND what it replaces — innovation is relative, and the reader needs to understand what the current approach is to appreciate why yours is better. Every claim about resources, personnel, or timeline should connect to a specific scientific goal — reviewers who can't trace logistics to science flag it as unfocused.
persona.dealbreakers: Overpromising results without acknowledging risk, jargon-heavy prose that excludes non-specialist reviewers, passive voice that hides who is doing the work, unfocused aims that try to do too much
persona.initial_task: Help me draft the opening paragraph and first specific aim (2-3 paragraphs total) for an NIH R01 proposal on using wearable biosensors to detect early signs of cognitive decline in aging populations — we have pilot data from a 60-person study showing our gait variability metric detects changes 8 months before standard cognitive screening, and our innovation replaces the current reliance on annual in-clinic assessments.
tasks: Help me draft the opening paragraph and first specific aim (2-3 paragraphs total) for an NIH R01 proposal on using wearable biosensors to detect early signs of cognitive decline in aging populations — we have pilot data from a 60-person study showing our gait variability metric detects changes 8 months before standard cognitive screening, and our innovation replaces the current reliance on annual in-clinic assessments.

I need to write the significance and innovation sections (2-3 paragraphs total) for an NSF proposal on using machine learning to predict wildfire spread from satellite imagery — our innovation is a real-time model that updates predictions every 15 minutes instead of the current 6-hour cycle. We have pilot data from the 2024 California fire season showing our model outperformed the existing FARSITE system.

Help me draft the opening and first specific aim (2-3 paragraphs) for an NIH R21 proposal on using gut microbiome profiling to personalize dietary interventions for Type 2 diabetes — the current approach treats all patients the same, but our pilot data from 40 participants shows that microbiome composition predicts which patients respond to low-carb vs. Mediterranean diets.

I need to write the significance section and second specific aim (2-3 paragraphs) for a DOE proposal on developing perovskite-silicon tandem solar cells — current single-junction silicon cells have hit a practical efficiency ceiling around 26%, and our lab has demonstrated a tandem architecture reaching 29% in bench tests. The aim focuses on scaling from lab-scale to commercial-size panels.

Help me draft the opening paragraph and innovation section (2-3 paragraphs) for an NSF CAREER proposal on using natural language processing to detect early signs of depression from social media language patterns — our approach replaces keyword-matching with contextual models that capture changes in a user's own baseline, and we have IRB-approved pilot data from 200 consenting participants.
